coredata.dc:title: S doped silicon quantum dots with high quantum yield as a fluorescent sensor for determination of Fe3+ in water
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaoshuo
Li, Chunhua
Zhao, Sheliang
Pang, Huiyun
Han, Yong
Luo, Xueli
Tang, Wenzhi
Li, Zhonghong
coredata.dc:description: Novel S-doped silicon quantum dots (S-SiQDs) were prepared by l -cysteine doped silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) for fluorescent detecting Fe3+ in water. The as-prepared S-SiQDs sensor with an average diameter of 4.77 nm possessed richness of –NH2, endowing good water solubility and strong blue fluorescence. The quantum yield (QY) of ~66% was abtained when S-SiQDs exited at 345 nm. The synergistic effect of electron transfer that the electron of –NH2 on S-SiQDs surface transfer to 3d unoccupied obrials of Fe3+, inner filter effectaccounted for fluorescence quenching. The probe possessed a relative wide liner range from 1 μmol/L to 20 μmol/L with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.21 μmol/L to detecte Fe3+. The as-prepared S-SiQDs sensor could sensitively and selectively detect Fe3+ in real water samples with satisfied recoveries of 90%–106%, which presented its potential applicability in the environmental fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon quantum dots
Trivalent iron ion
Optical sensor
Fluorescent probe
Quantum yield

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and purification of S-SiQDs sensor
The feasibility of S-SiQDs as the fluorescence probe for Fe3+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31801628 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: S-SiQDs as the fluorescent probe for detecting Fe3+.

(A) TEM image of S-SiQDs (inset: the particle size distribution of S-SiQDs); (B) FT-IR spectra of l -cysteine, SiQDs, S-SiQDs and S-SiQDs + Fe3+; High resolution XPS spectra of full range of (C) C 1s, (D) N 1s, (E) O 1s, (F) Si 2p, and (G) S 2p peak of the S-SiQDs; (H) UV–vis absorption of S-SiQDs (a) and Fe3+ (b), fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of S-SiQDs (c, e) and SiQDs (d, f) (insets: (left) and (right) were S-SiQDs before and after excited with 365 nm UV lamp); (I) QY of S-SiQDs.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of S-SiQDs at different pH; (B) Incubation time versus fluorescence intensity of S-SiQDs, the F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of 40 μmol/L Fe3+; (C) Fluorescence intensity of S-SiQDs versus NaCl different concentrations; (D) Fluorescence intensity of S-SiQDs versus different temperatures.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of S-SiQDs upon addition of Fe3+ with different concentrations; (B) The plot of the fluorescence quenching factor ((F0–F)/F0) versus concentrations of Fe3+. (inset: relevant linear regions).
Fluorescence intensity of S-SiQDs with 20 μmol/L Fe3+ and interfering species (100-fold concentration of Fe3+) in the absence of Fe3+.

(A) Suppressed efficiency (E, %) of observed and corrected fluorescence intensity; (B) High resolution XPS spectra of N 1s of S-SiQDs before and after adding Fe3+; (C) Fluorescence decay spectra of S-SiQDs before and after incubating the Fe3+.
Comparison of different fluorescence methods for Fe3+ sensing.

row.entry: Low sensitivity, complicated process to synthesize the probe
Low quantum yield, not environmentally friendly (introduction of heavy metal)
Time-consuming, not environmentally friendly (introduction of heavy metal)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Fe3+ in real water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: S doped silicon quantum dots with high quantum yield as a fluorescent sensor for determination of Fe3+ in water

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel S-doped silicon quantum dots (S-SiQDs) were prepared by l -cysteine doped silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) for fluorescent detecting Fe3+ in water. The as-prepared S-SiQDs sensor with an average diameter of 4.77 nm possessed richness of –NH2, endowing good water solubility and strong blue fluorescence. The quantum yield (QY) of ~66% was abtained when S-SiQDs exited at 345 nm. The synergistic effect of electron transfer that the electron of –NH2 on S-SiQDs surface transfer to 3d unoccupied obrials of Fe3+, inner filter effectaccounted for fluorescence quenching. The probe possessed a relative wide liner range from 1 μmol/L to 20 μmol/L with the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.21 μmol/L to detecte Fe3+. The as-prepared S-SiQDs sensor could sensitively and selectively detect Fe3+ in real water samples with satisfied recoveries of 90%–106%, which presented its potential applicability in the environmental fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Trivalent iron ion (Fe3+), an important component of human body, plays an essential role in many biochemical processes, such as cell metabolism, enzyme catalysis, electron transfer, oxidation reaction and oxygen transport [1]. The deficiency of Fe3+ in primary stages may cause anaemia and diabetes as well as liver and kidney damages. On the other hand, the excessive concentration of Fe3+ in human body may lead to coronary heart disease, cancer and immune system dysfunction [2,3]. Moreover, in environmental quality assessment, the content of Fe3+ is an important indicator to evaluate water quality. The US Environmental Protection Agency has established that the LOD was lower than the maximum level (0.3 mg/L, equivalent to 5.4 μmol/L) of Fe3+ permitted in drinking water [4,5]. Therefore, it is of great significance to detect the amount of Fe3+ in water. To date, many methods have been employed for Fe3+ sensing, e.g. inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [6], voltammetry [7], liquid chromatography [8] and atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [9]. Although these methods are quantitative, they are usually complicated, time consuming and expensive. All in all, it is necessary to establish a fast and simple method for detecting Fe3+.

In recent years, fluorescent method has attracted much attention for detecting Fe3+ because of their unique and excellent optical properties, which uses ﬂuorescent materials (organic ﬂuorescent dyes or ﬂuorescent nanomaterials) as a detection platform. Fluorescent nanomaterials such as quantum dots (QDs) [10], metallic nanoclusters (MNC) [11] and fluorescent metal−organic frameworks (FMOFs) [12] are becoming potential new platforms in designing fluorescent probes. However, their wide applications are affected by inherent defects. For example, MNC are easily aggregated because of their poor stability. Besides, FMOFs possess relative low hydrothermal stability and chemical stability, and the skeleton is prone to collapse [13]. Among these fluorescent materials QDs have become the focus of nanomaterials because of their special photoelectric properties, excellent biocompatibility and stability [14]. At present, QDs are usually divided into III-V QDs (InAs, GaSb, GaN) [15], II-VI QDs (CdSe, ZnSe, CdTe) [16] and IV QDs (Si, Ge) [17]. Compared with traditional III-V and II-VI QDs, silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) have unique properties which are innocuous, environmentally friendly, low-cost, high sensitivity and selectivity. As the sensors, SiQDs are commonly used in the detection of metal ions (Cr6+, Hg2+, Ag+) [18,19] or organic molecules (heparin, miRNA) [20,21] owing to their outstanding properties including multicolour emission, biocompatibility, multifunctional groups, water dispersability and excellent photostability. However, the reported SiQDs mostly show low QY generally less than 25%, which significantly restricts their practical applications [22–24]. Recently, doping SiQDs with heteroatom can break the chemical inertness of silicon matrix by disrupting the electronic and spin culture of the sp2-hybridized silicon because of its own atomic radius and orbit, electronegativity and electron density [25,26]. Among the alien atoms, S is recognized as a preeminent dopant to create defects. Doping S with larger size and smaller electronegativity is beneficial for further expanding the interlayer distance, creating active sites, and significantly boosting electronic properties of silicon materials [27–30]. As a transition metal with strong electron-donating ability, Fe3+ can be captured by oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g. –NH2) of CDs and thus change the surface state of CDs, leading to FL quenching of CDs [31]. To the best of our knowledge, the FL assay to detect Fe3+ based on SiQDs nanosensor has not been reported yet.

In this work, we chose APTES as silicon nitrogen-rich source so that the residual –NH2 can serve as recognition sites of Fe3+ and trisodium citrate as reduction reagent to prepare SiQDs through one-step hydrothermal method. As we all known, l -cysteine was a kind of sulfur-containing amino acid. So we chose l -cysteine as the dopant composed of S-doped SiQDs. The as-prepared S-SiQDs existed strong blue fluorescence, good water solubility and fluorescent stability. It was found that in the presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence could be quenched by Fe3+ (Fig. 1 ). Therefore, S-SiQDs acted as a fluorescent probe for quantitative analysis of Fe3+ in water samples.

All chemicals were used of analytical reagent grade without additional purification. Trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7.3H2O) and anhydrous sodium acetate (CH3COONa) were purchased from Tianjin Bodi Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2.6H2O) and lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2) were obtained from Chengdu Kelong Co. Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Sulphuric acid (H2SO4) and ethylic acid (CH3COOH) were obtained from Xilong Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Aluminium hexahydrate chloride (AlCl3.6H2O) was obtained from Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloridean hydrous (CaCl2.6H2O), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O) and sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Kermel Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjing, China). Copper dichloride (CuCl2.2H2O), quinine sulfate (C20H24N2O2), chromic chloride (CrCl3) and manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2.4H2O) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was purchased from Shandong Kebang Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shandong, China). (3-Aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) was purchased from Shanghai Aijie Biological Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water (DW) was used through all experiments.

Transimission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded by JEM-2100 miscroscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) to analyze the elemental and chemical states (Kratos, UK). UV–vis spectra were obtained by using a Shimadzu UV–vis 2550 Spectrophotomer (Japan). All fluorescence measurements were measured on a LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, America) using a 1 cm path length quartz cuvette, scanning from 375 to 575 nm at room temperature. FT-IR spectra were carried out with a Vetex 70 FTIR Spectroscope (Bruker Crop, Germany) and cumulated 32 scans at resolution of 4 cm−1. The quantum yield (QY) of SiQDs was calculated with quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as reference standard, which was newly prepared to reduce the measurement error.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and purification of S-SiQDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
SiQDs were firstly synthesized by one-step hydrothermal synthesis had been previously reported by He et al. with a certain modification. Normally, 0.368 g of trisodium citrate was dissolved in 8 mL deionized water and oxygen needed to be removed with nitrogen bubbles for about 5 min. Then 2 mL APTES was injected into the solution above. When the above solution was completely dissolved, 0.092 g l
-cysteine was added into the mixture. Under the flask with sustainable aeration of nitrogen, the precursor of S-SiQDs was formed by homogeneously stirring for 10 min. Then the precursor solution was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave lined with tetrafluoroethylene and incubated in an oven. The reaction time and temperature were 2 h and 200 °C, respectively. In order to remove the redundant impurities, including APTES, trisodium citrate and l
-cysteine at the end of the preparation, the solution was purified by 500–1000 D dialysis bag for 3 days, and the dialyzed S-SiQDs solution was collected and stored at 4 °C for use.

The prepared S-SiQDs was diluted 100-fold for the fluorescence measurements unless stated otherwise. When detected Fe3+, 100 μL as-prepared S-SiQDs solution, 100 μL acetate buffer solution (pH = 5, 0.01 mol/L) and different concentrations of Fe3+ solution were mixed and then diluted to 1.5 mL with deionized water. The finally obtained solution was incubated at room temperature for 30 s before fluorescence measurements. The fluorescent spectra were recorded under from 375 to 575 nm. Several experimental conditions were carried out to optimize the detection parameters. The selectivity of the S-SiQDs probe towards Fe3+ was confirmed upon adding different metal ions instead of Fe3+.

For the further applications of S-SiQDs sensor for Fe3+ determination, tap water, mineral water and river water were tested via standard addition method. The content of Fe3+ in the samples was obtained from the linear function according a standard solution of Fe3+. Tap water and mineral water samples were collected from a local factory, and river water samples were collected from the Weihe River in Yangling, Shaanxi province. All water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane without any other sample pre-treatment. The fluorescence spectra of solutions were recorded under excitation at 345 nm. Three times of fluorescence intensity were measured and recorded as a mean in all solutions.

The characterization of S-SiQDs sensor included morphology characterization, surface functional groups and optical characterization (absorption, excitation, emission and QY). The morphology of the as-prepared S-SiQDs was studied by the TEM image. As shown in TEM image (Fig. 2 A), S-SiQDs appeared as spherical particles. The inset of Fig. 2A showed the size of S-SiQDs was about 1.37–7.95 nm with an average diameter of 4.77 nm. FT-IR spectra of l -cysteine, SiQDs and S-SiQDs were shown in Fig. 2B to obtain the chemical and structural information. With respect to S-SiQDs, the absorbance peak at 3425 cm−1 was related to stretching vibrations. The strong absorbance at 1639 and 1692 cm−1 were assigned to N–H bending vibration. These results demonstrated that S-SiQDs were surrounded by a lot of –NH2. The presence of symmetric stretching vibrations of C–N could be evidenced by the apparent peak at 1404 cm−1. The peak of C–H stretching vibration was observed at around 2935 cm−1 [32]. The absorption peak at 1155 cm−1 implied the existence of C–O group. The peaks at 2561 and 995 cm−1 demonstrated the S–H and C–S of grapheme, which was unique for S-SiQDs to distinguish from SiQDs [33,34]. In S-SiQDs curve, the absorption peak at 1077 cm−1 shifted to 1045 cm−1 compared SiQDs. The peak located at 1077 cm−1 may be ascribed to Si–O/SO groups, while the peak at 1045 cm−1 could be ascribed to Si–O groups [23,24]. The groups of SO, S–H and C–S existed which illustrated that S atom was successfully doped into S-SiQDs. FT-IR analysis showed that S-SiQDs were rich in hydrophilic groups. These hydrophilic groups were distributed on the surfaces, helping to stabilize S-SiQDs in aqueous media. The abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface of S-SiQDs provided a rich binding site for Fe3+. Moreover, these functional groups would produce defects on the surface of S-SiQDs and become excitation energy traps and thus produce excellent fluorescence performance [35].

The XRD pattern of theproduct is displayed in Fig. S1. There was a diffraction peak with the range of 26.30°–32.30°, which corresponded to a silicon structure. And the reason of this broadening of the diffraction peak was the small size of S-SiQDs. To further analyze S-SiQDs, XPS measurements were performed (Fig. S2). The high resolution C1s XPS spectrum of S-SiQDs (Fig. 2C) revealed the following six types of carbon atoms: C–S (283.97eV), C–Si (284.37eV), C–C (284.77eV), C–N (285.27eV), C–O (285.97eV) and C–O–C (287.87eV) groups, respectively [36]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) was composed of three peaks centred at 399.17eV (C–N–C), 399.97eV (N–(C)3) and 401.37eV (N–H), suggesting that N existed in three forms [37]. This indicated that the as-prepared S-SiQDs were rich in –NH2 on the surfaces, which was consistent with the corresponding FT-IR spectra. Two peaks at 531.41 and 532.05 eV presaged the presence of Si–O (Fig. 2E). The other peak at 532.49 eV were assigned to C–O. The binding energy of Si 2p (Fig. 2F) was observed at 99.82, 102.33 and 102.91 eV, which were in accordance with Si–C, Si–N and Si–O groups, respectively [32]. The presence of S 2p (Fig. 2G) indicated two peaks centred at 163.91 and 168.75 eV could be ascribed to C–S and SO groups [38]. The XPS results conﬁrmed that S-SiQDs with good water solubility were successfully synthesized, which was consistent with FT-IR spectra.

The UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of S-SiQDs were exhibited in Fig. 2H. Only one prominent absorption peak at 345 nm was observed, which was ascribed to the n–π* transition and the contribution of the energy trap derived from the heteroatom passivated surface state [39]. This absorption and the presence of surface states can lead to strong emission features in doped S-SiQDs. This result was consistent with the optimal fluorescence excitation peak at 345 nm. It could be seen that the emission fluorescence peak of the S-SiQDs (Em = 425 nm) had a blue-shift with the doping of S atom in comparison with that of free SiQDs (Em = 429 nm). The as-prepared S-SiQDs exhibited a large Stokes shift of 80 nm compared the absorption with excitation spectrum, indicating high detection sensitivity for analytical applications [31]. While there was no fluorescence was seen in S-SiQDs solution under visible light (left of inset of Fig. 2H), blue fluorescence was observed when irradiated in the presence of UV light (365 nm) (right of inset of Fig. 1H). Fig. S3 displayed the fluorescence emission spectra of S-SiQDs with different excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 370 nm. These fluorescence intensities corresponded from 215 to 802 and the strongest peak arose at 802 with excitation wavelength of 345 nm. Therefore, the S-SiQDs maximum excitation at 345 nm was selected for the further experiment. Fig. S4 showed that the fluorescence intensity didn't change during about 30 min irradiation with low pressure mercury lamp, which proved the stability of S-SiQDs solution.

Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2I, the as-prepared S-SiQDs displayed good photoluminescent behaviour with the ~66% of QY using quinine sulfate (QY = 54%) as a reference standard, which was improved compared to the previous report. The high QY could be ascribed to the presence of hetero atom S, introducing a new type of surface state (C–S, S–H and SO). Generally, hydroxyl and other oxygen-containing groups were easy to induce local electron-hole formation without radiation recombination. Doping heteroatom S would consume some of the oxygen-containing functional groups and the probability of the non-radiative recombination generation was reduced, resulting that the QY of S-SiQDs was improved [40]. Furthermore, the calculated QY was a satisfactory for ﬂuorescent probes to be used as fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of Fe3+ with excellent sensitivity.

Before measurements of impact quenching performance of Fe3+ to S-SiQDs, several experimental conditions were systematically explored, which included the pH of detection, the incubation time, ionic strength, the effect of temperature on the fluorescence properties and the stability of fluorescence properties. The pH of the buffer solution had significant difference on the fluorescence intensity of the sensor. The results in Fig. 3 A showed that the fluorescence intensity of the system increased rapidly when the pH turned from 2.0 to 5.0. The fluorescence intensity reached the maximum at pH of 5.0 and scarcely showed any change in the pH range from 5.0 to 10.0. Fe3+ was easy to form insoluble iron hydroxide because of its poor stability in alkaline medium [41]. Taking this factor into account, pH of 5.0 was selected for subsequent experiments. The variation of fluorescence intensity with incubation time was given in Fig. 3B. The fluorescence intensity of S-SiQDs decreased quickly after adding 0.67 mmol/L Fe3+ and tended to stabilize after 30 s. It was shown that there were enough functional groups located on S-SiQDs to interact rapidly with Fe3+, which led to the quenching of fluorescence. Therefore, fluorescence incubation time of 30 s was selected in the subsequent experiment. It can be seen from Fig. 3C that there was no obvious effect of ionic strengths on ﬂuorescence intensity (the concentrations of NaCl from 0 to 1 mol/L), indicating that S-SiQDs had the potential to detect Fe3+ in physiological environments. From Fig. 3D, the temperature (4–60 °C) had no obvious effect on the fluorescence intensity of the S-SiQDs sensor. In addition, there was no significant decrease in fluorescence intensity within 30 days, which showed a good stability of fluorescence. All above observations demonstrated excellent stability of S-SiQDs probe towards Fe3+ in ambient environment. Based on these discoveries, the analyte system was incubated for 30 s after adding Fe3+ under the pH of 5.0 before fluorescence measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of S-SiQDs as the fluorescence probe for Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on these discoveries, we evaluated the ability of the analytical system to carry out quantitative detection of Fe3+. The linearity and LOD of the sensor system were studied. The fluorescence emission spectra of S-SiQDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ were shown in Fig. 4 A. The system clearly reflected the quenching effect of the addition of Fe3+ on the fluorescence. Fluorescence quenching factor versus the concentrations of Fe3+ was used to calculate regression equation according to Eq. (1).
(1)
(
F 0
− F
)
/
F 0
= a
[ C ]
+ b
where F0 and F corresponded to the fluorescence intensity of solution before and after the addition of Fe3+, respectively. C was the concentration of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 4B, when Fe3+ of different concentrations were added to the S-SiQDs probe solution, it was apparent that the fluorescence intensity of S-SiQDs decreased continuously with increasing the concentration of Fe3+ and trended to be invariablenes at a certain concentration. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4B, the linear relationship between the concentration of (F
0
–F)/F
0 and C was (F
0
–F)/F
0 = 0.02365 + 0.01228 [C] (μmol/L). And the range of detection Fe3+ was from 1 to 20 μmol/L with a correlation coefficient (0.9995). The LOD was calculated to be 0.21 μmol/L, where LOD was estimated according to IUPAC criterion (3σ/k). As shown in Table 1 , compared with other fluorescence probe for determination of Fe3+, S-SiQDs probe had the advantages of high selectivity, low LOD, fast response, good operation security and simple preparation of materials.

To investigate the specificity of the S-SiQDs probe for detection of Fe3+ in water samples, the effects of other relevant metal irons were carried out under the optimum experimental conditions. As we can see from Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensities after adding other metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Pb2+, Zn2+) were observed in the same sensing system. Due to the surface doping of the S heteroatom with low electronegativity, electron-deficient Fe3+ had a higher binding affinity towards the electron-rich –NH2 groups compared with other metal ions, which disrupted the initial electron transition of the S-SiQDs but was beneficial for the transfer of the excited electrons on S-SiQDs to the unfilled orbital of Fe3+ [42]. In a closer look, it could be seen that other than Fe3+, Cr3+also somewhat changed the fluorescence intensity of S-SiQDs. Due to the three positive charges of Cr3+, there occurred interaction with function groups. However, the fluorescence quenching in presence of Cr3+ was negligible compared to Fe3+. The results above illustrated that the S-SiQDs sensor had an excellent selectivity and strong tolerance for Fe3+ and could be used in the following Fe3+ sensing.

Usually, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), the inner filter effect (IFE) and the electron transfer (ET) could lead to the fluorescence quenching of fluorescent materials by Fe3+. Thus, the quenching mechanism was considered firstly to come from FRET. In order to further study this hypothesis, the fluorescence emission of S-SiQDs and the absorption characteristics of Fe3+ solutions were considered. As presented in Fig. 2H, there was no obvious spectral overlap between absorption peak of Fe3+ and emission peak of S-SiQDs, so FRET was not part of the fluorescence quenching mechanism.

Meanwhile, it was obviously shown that Fe3+ had an absorption peak at about 308 nm, which was overlapped with the excitation peak (345 nm) of S-SiQDs with a wide range of 250–350 nm. This indicated that the fluorescence of S-SiQDs was quenched by Fe3+ because of IFE. The IFE and quenching efficiency could be estimated according to Eq (2) and Eq (3) [43].
(2)
F
c o r r
/
F
o b e d
= 2.3 d
A
e x
/
(
1 −
10
− d
A
e x
)
∗
2.3 s
A
e m
10
g
A
e m
/
(
1 −
10
− d
A
e m
)
(3)
Q E
( % )
=
(
F 0
− F
)
/
F 0
∗ 100
where Fobsd and Fcorr were the maximum fluorescence intensity of measured and corrected after removing the IFE, respectively. Aex and Aem were the absorbance intensity of the detection system at wavelengths of 345 nm and 425 nm d was the width of the cuvette (1.00 cm), s denoted the thickness of the excitation beam (0.10 cm), and g represented the distance of the edge of the excitation beam and the cuvette (0.40 cm). Moreover, F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensities of the S-SiQDs sensor before and after Fe3+ introduction. Fig. 6 A showed the quenching efficiency of observed (Eobsd) and corrected (Ecor) fluorescence of S-SiQDs with different concentrations of Fe3+. It was calculated that the suppressed efficiency of IFE for Fe3+ to S-SiQDs was about ~33.5% of the total quenching efficiency.

Moreover, we thought that the residual quenching effect was likely to stem from the ET. The UV absorption spectra of S-SiQDs and S-SiQDs + Fe3+ were scanned respectively. As shown in Fig. S5, we could see that S-SiQDs had a wide absorption peak at 345 nm, and the absorption intensity increased gradually with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. These results showed that the surface groups of S-SiQDs and Fe3+ were coordinated to form complexes. From the FT-IR of S-SiQDs + Fe3+ (Fig. 2B), it had been seen that the absorption peak of –NH2 at the wavenumber of 1560 cm−1 had disappeared compared with the curve of S-SiQDs. At the same time, the peaks at 1639 cm−1corresponding to N–H bending vibrations moved to 1631 cm−1 in the FT-IR curve of S-SiQDs + Fe3+. Meanwhile, the peak at 1506 cm−1 disappeared when Fe3+ was added into S-SiQDs. These changes indicated that Fe3+ coordinated with amine groups on the surface of S-SiQDs. In addition, the N 1s spectrum changed before and after addition Fe3+. The percentage content of N–H changed from 42.75% to 20.52% comparing to the pure S-SiQDs (Fig. 6B). These results further confirmed that Fe3+ combined –NH2 forming complexes. Because Fe3+ was a paramagnetic transition metal ion with semi-filled 3d orbit, the possible quenching mechanism was that Fe3+, as an effective electron acceptor or hole, caused non-radiative photo induced ET. The functional groups on the surface of S-SiQDs could coordinate with Fe3+ to form complexes, which made the excited state electrons of S-SiQDs transition to the semi-filled 3d empty orbitals of Fe3+, which led to quench fluorescence [44]. According to literature [45], although both N and S atoms were electron donors, the electronegativity of S atom (2.58) was small than N atom (3.05) and the valence electrons of S in the third shell tended to lose easily. Therefore, the introduction of S atom into SiQDs could effectively regulate the electron density of S-SiQDs and promote the coordination between –NH2 on the S-SiQDs surface and Fe3+. Rapid ET promoted Fe3+ to effectively quench the fluorescence of S-SiQDs. In order to get the complete quenching mechanism, time-resolved fluorescence data were obtained before and after incubating the Fe3+, as shown in Fig. 6C. The calculated average life time of the S-SiQDs and S-SiQDs/Fe3+ was found to be 9.71 ns and 9.18 ns, respectively. Negligible change in fluorescence lifetime suggested the transfer of electrons from the excited state of S-SiQDs to the unfilled orbital of Fe3+, leading to a nonradiative electron transfer process. [46,47]. In conclusion, the fluorescence of S-SiQDs can be quenched by Fe3+ via IFE, ET, static quenching and promoted interaction pathway.

To explore the utility of the probe, the application of the S-SiQDs probe for Fe3+ sensing in water was studied further. The accuracy of the proposed method was verified by determining the quenching rate of Fe3+. The results of the standard addition method were summarized in Table 2 . The relative standard deviation (RSD) was lower than 2.14% for Fe3+. The recoveries of Fe3+ ranged from 90% to 106%, indicating the validity of the S-SiQDs probe for Fe3+ determination. These results further proved the reliability and feasibility of this probe that was suitable for conventional analysis.

In the present work, a new silicon quantum dots doped with l -cysteine (S-SiQDs) probe was presented with high QY (~66.21%). This new S-SiQDs synthesis strategy provided an excellent way to increase the QY of SiQDs by heteroatom doping S. The S-SiQDs probe could form complexes with Fe3+ resulting in strong fluorescence quenching. The sensor exhibited fast fluorescence quenching response, excellent selectivity and high sensitivity. Therefore, this probe could be successfully used to detect Fe3+ in water samples with acceptable accuracy and precision. Benefiting from the unique characteristics of the S-SiQDs probe, this method would have great potential for using as a sensing platform for the determination of Fe3+ in environmental fields.

Xiaoshuo Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology. Chunhua Li: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Sheliang Zhao: Visualization, Investigation. Huiyun Pang: Supervision. Yong Han: Writing - original draft, Validation. Xueli Luo: Writing - review & editing. Wenzhi Tang: Project administration. Zhonghong Li: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31801628).
